content: The Krebs-Henseleit cycle of urea formation is a sequence of biochemical reactions that takes place in___________
explanation: The urea cycle takes place in hepatocytes (liver cells) to convert ammonia into urea.

content:
People who don't drink enough water and consume high protein diet, may suffer from

__________ kidney stones.
explanation:
People who don’t drink enough water and consume a high-protein diet may suffer from uric acid kidney stones.

✅ Correct answer: Option B — uric acid

Explanation:

  • A high-protein diet (especially rich in animal protein) increases the production of uric acid from purine metabolism.

  • Low water intake leads to concentrated urine, which promotes crystal formation.

  • Together, these factors increase the risk of uric acid stone formation.

content:
Renal medulla contains __________ .

i. Malpighian bodies and convoluted tubules

ii. blood vessels and glomerulus

iii. Loop of Henle

iv. Collecting ducts
explanation: Renal Cortex contains Malpighian bodies, convoluted tubules and blood vessels.

options.content: constricting blood vessels and increase in BP

explanation:
The correct answer is:

✅ Option C: forming active Angiotensin II

Explanation:

Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) plays a key role in the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system (RAAS). It converts angiotensin I (inactive) into angiotensin II (active), which then leads to:

  • Vasoconstriction (narrowing of blood vessels)

  • Increased blood pressure

  • Stimulation of aldosterone secretion from the adrenal cortex

Thus, ACE itself forms active angiotensin II; the effects such as blood vessel constriction occur due to angiotensin II.

content: Which of the following is INCORRECT in case of severe haemorrhage?

options.content: ADH stimulates reabsorption of water from last part of DCT and entire collecting duct.

explanation: In severe haemorrhage, ADH stimulates increased water reabsorption and leads to reduced urine volume.

content:
Match the Columns I, II and III based an excretion.

Column I
(Part of Nephron)		 Column II
(Type of cells)		 Column III
(Function)
a. Bownman's capsule (i) Squamous epithelium p. passage of filterate
b. Neck of nephron (ii) Cuboidal epithelium q. selective reabsorption
c. Proximal convoluted tubule (iii) Ciliated epithelium r. filtration of blood

options.content: $\quad a-i i-p, b-i-q, c-i i i-r$
$\quad a-i-r, b-i i i-p, c-i i-q$
a - ii - q, b - iii - p, c-i - r

explanation:
1. Bowman's capsule (a):

    • Type of cells (Column II): The outer wall (parietal layer) of Bowman's capsule is composed of simple squamous epithelium. The inner wall (visceral layer) consists of podocytes, which are specialized epithelial cells, but overall, squamous epithelium (i) is a good general description for the filtration barrier.

    • Function (Column III): Bowman's capsule, together with the glomerulus, is the site where blood filtration occurs, forming the glomerular filtrate. So, its function is filtration of blood (r).

    • Therefore, a - i - r.

  2. Neck of nephron (b):

    • Type of cells (Column II): The neck region, which connects Bowman's capsule to the PCT, is often described as being lined by ciliated epithelium (iii), especially in the early nephron models studied (e.g., in amphibians) and sometimes in human nephrons to aid initial flow.

    • Function (Column III): The cilia, if present, would help in the initial movement or passage of filtrate (p) from the Bowman's capsule into the proximal convoluted tubule.

    • Therefore, b - iii - p.

  3. Proximal convoluted tubule (c):

    • Type of cells (Column II): The PCT is characterized by a lining of simple cuboidal epithelium (ii) cells. These cells have a prominent brush border (microvilli) on their apical surface, significantly increasing the surface area for reabsorption.

    • Function (Column III): The PCT is the primary site for the reabsorption of a large percentage (about 65-70%) of water, electrolytes, glucose, amino acids, and other beneficial substances from the filtrate back into the blood. This is a highly regulated and selective reabsorption (q) process.

    • Therefore, c - ii - q.

Combining these matches, we get:

  • a - i - r

  • b - iii - p

  • c - ii - q

Now let's compare this with the given options:

  • Option A: a-i-r, b-ii-q, c-iii-p (Incorrect)

  • Option B: a-ii-p, b-i-q, c-iii-r (Incorrect)

  • Option C: a-i-r, b-iii-p, c-ii-q (Correct)

  • Option D: a-ii-q, b-iii-p, c-i-r (Incorrect)

The final answer is $\boxed{C}$

content: Primary urine contains all of the following, EXCEPT

options.content: Blood cells and macro molecules like proteins

explanation: Primary urine (glomerular filtrate) lacks blood cells and large proteins due to filtration barriers in the glomerulus.

content:
Which of the following is correct regarding cystine stones?

i. These are formed in response to bacterial infection.

ii. It is a genetic disorder.

iii. These grow quickly and become quite large.

iv. Occurs in people who consume high protein diet.

v. People who suffer excrete too much of certain amino acid.
explanation: Cystine stones result from a genetic disorder causing excess amino acid excretion, not bacterial infection or diet.

content: In oliguria condition, urine output is less than ___________.
explanation: Oliguria - scanty urine. A decrease in urine output (less than $400 \mathrm{ml} /$ day or less than 0.5 $\mathrm{ml} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{h}$ in children) indicates kidney dysfunction.

content: Which of the following pairs of organisms are ureotelic?
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text { Ureotelic } & \begin{array}{l} \text { Aquatic reptiles, most of Adult } \\ \text { amphibians, mammals } \end{array} \\ \hline \text { Ammoniotelic } & \text { Osteichthyes (Bony Fishes) } \\ \hline \text { Uricotelic } & \text { Land snails, Birds } \\ \hline \end{array} $$

content: Most energy saving mode of excretion is_________.
explanation:
Let us analyse step by step:

  1. Excretion is the removal of nitrogenous wastes from the body.

  2. Different animals use different modes:

  • Ammonotelism: Excreting ammonia. Requires very large amount of water, hence not energy efficient.

  • Ureotelism: Excreting urea. Requires some energy for conversion of ammonia → urea, but conserves water.

  • Uricotelism: Excreting uric acid. Conversion requires more energy, but it conserves maximum water because uric acid is almost insoluble and excreted as paste/semisolid.

  • Guanotelism: Excreting guanine. Very rare, seen in some spiders.

  1. The term "energy saving" in NCERT typically refers to water conservation vs. energy cost.

  • Ammonotelism = least energy used, but most water needed.

  • Uricotelism = most energy used, but minimum water lost.

  1. Question is asking: "Most energy saving mode" i.e., the mode that requires least ATP to convert ammonia.

✅ Therefore, the correct answer is:

Option A - Ammonotelism

content: Kidneys are NOT associated with secretion of
explanation:
The correct answer is:

✅ Option B — antidiuretic hormone (ADH)

Explanation:

The kidneys are involved in the secretion or production of several important substances:

  • Erythropoietin (EPO): Produced by the kidneys; stimulates red blood cell production in the bone marrow.

  • Renin: Secreted by the juxtaglomerular cells of the kidney; helps regulate blood pressure via the renin–angiotensin–aldosterone system.

  • Calcitriol (active form of vitamin D₃): Produced by the kidneys; important for calcium and phosphate balance.

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH):

  • Is not secreted by the kidneys.

  • It is produced by the hypothalamus and released from the posterior pituitary gland.

  • It acts on the kidneys to increase water reabsorption.

Therefore, kidneys are NOT associated with secretion of ADH.

content: Juxta glomerular apparatus in kidney releases Renin, when a person is __________
explanation:
Juxta Glomerular Apparatus (JGA) functions as a regulator of blood pressure and glomerular filtration rate.

  • JGA releases Renin when there is a fall in blood pressure or blood volume.

  • Renin converts angiotensinogen into angiotensin I, which ultimately increases blood pressure and blood volume.

Now, check the options:

  • Option A: Dehydrated and has high BP → Incorrect, because renin is not released in high BP.

  • Option B: Hydrated and with high BP → Incorrect, same reason.

  • Option C: Hydrated and with low BP → Incorrect, because low BP usually occurs with dehydration.

  • Option D: Dehydrated and with low BP → Correct, because low BP due to dehydration stimulates JGA to release renin.

Final Answer: Option D – dehydrated and with low BP ✅

Let us check each option one by one:

Option A: Infection to kidney → increased leucocytes

  • In case of kidney infection, pus cells (leucocytes) are present in urine.

  • This is correct.

Option B: Renal calculi → increased calcium oxalate

  • Renal calculi (kidney stones) are often formed due to deposition of calcium oxalate.

  • This is correct.

Option C: Nephritis → increased glucose in urine

  • Nephritis is the inflammation of nephrons.

  • The main symptom is presence of proteins or blood cells in urine, not glucose.

  • Glucose is found in urine mainly in diabetes mellitus (glycosuria).

  • Hence, this statement is incorrect.

Option D: Albuminuria → increased protein in urine

  • Albuminuria means presence of albumin (a protein) in urine.

  • This is correct.

Final Answer:

The incorrect pair is

Option C: Nephritis – increased glucose in urine ✅

content:
Which of the following are true about sharks?

i. Their body fluids is isosmotic to external environment.

ii. They are osmoconformers.

iii. They control their internal environment independent of external environment.

iv. They are osmoregulators.
explanation: Animals can either be isoosmotic to the surrounding (osmoconformers) or control internal environment independent of external environment (osmoregulators). Marine organisms mostly are osmocomformers because their body fluids and external environment are isoosmotic in nature.

content:
Given below are two statements.

Statement I - Defecation is an involuntary process that takes place through anal opening guarded by sphincter muscles.

Statement II - Distension, of rectum stimulates pressure sensitive receptors that initiate a neural reflex for egestion.

In light of above statements, select the correct answer from the option given below.

options.content: Both statement I and statement II are correct.
Both statement I and statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct and statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect and statement II is correct.

explanation:
1. Defecation reflex and voluntary control

  • When faeces enter the rectum, stretch (pressure) receptors in its wall are activated.

  • This triggers a reflex via spinal cord (rectosphincteric reflex), causing the internal anal sphincter (smooth muscle) to relax.

  • The external anal sphincter (skeletal muscle) remains under voluntary control—so final expulsion requires conscious relaxation

  1. Analysis of Statement I

  • “Defecation is an involuntary process…”

    • Only the internal sphincter relaxation and peristalsis are involuntary.

    • The external sphincter must be voluntarily relaxed to allow faeces to pass.

    –→ Statement I is incorrect (defecation involves both involuntary reflex and voluntary control).

  1. Analysis of Statement II

  • “Distension of rectum stimulates pressure sensitive receptors that initiate a neural reflex for egestion.”

    –→ This correctly describes the rectal stretch receptors and the defecation reflex.

  1. Conclusion

    – Statement I: incorrect

    – Statement II: correct

Answer: Option D.

content: People who consume high protein diet can develop __________ stones.
explanation:
1. High-protein foods (meat, fish, eggs) are rich in purines.

  2. Purine metabolism increases uric acid production.

  3. Excess uric acid → more uric acid in urine + lower urine pH.

  4. In acidic urine, uric acid precipitates as crystals → uric acid stones.

Answer: Option B – uric acid stones.

content: An organ with sphincters at its origin is
explanation:
Sphincters are circular muscles that control the passage of fluid. At the junction of the bladder and the urethra there are two such sphincters:

Internal urethral sphincter

• Involuntary (smooth muscle)

• Keeps urine in the bladder

External urethral sphincter

• Voluntary (skeletal muscle)

• Allows conscious control of urination

Since these sphincters are located right at the start (origin) of the urethra, the correct answer is:

Option B: urethra

content: Tubular fluid tends to become acidic in the_________ of nephron.
explanation:
Step 1: Recall functions of different parts of nephron.

  • Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT): Reabsorbs glucose, amino acids, Na⁺, water, bicarbonate ($HCO_3^-$). Also secretes hydrogen ions ($H^+$).

  • Henle’s loop: Mainly concentrates urine by reabsorbing water (descending limb) and salts (ascending limb).

  • Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT): Important for fine regulation. Here, selective secretion of $H^+$ and $K^+$ occurs and reabsorption of $Na^+$ and water under hormonal control (aldosterone, ADH). Acid–base balance is adjusted here.

  • Neck: It is just a narrow tube connecting Bowman’s capsule to PCT, not involved in acidification.

Step 2: Identify where acidification occurs.

  • Acidification of urine (tubular fluid becoming acidic) takes place due to active secretion of $H^+$ ions.

  • This mainly occurs in the Distal Convoluted Tubule (DCT) and Collecting Duct, not in PCT or Henle’s loop.

Final Answer:

Option B: DCT ✅
